title: Thirty Minutes Over Tokyo

jokes.text: Hey, why didn't you tell me the new issue of Weird was here? [Chuckles] I love their hilarious send-ups of hit movies.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he's found his favorite magazine
jokes.punchline: He's completely wrong about what magazine it is
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer confusion - perfectly in character for him to misread the magazine title and be completely confident about it

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy! I found it! My favorite magazine!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's my "Better Homes and Gardens."

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, it's definitely mine. See? It has pictures of things I like.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Those are doilies and potpourri.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly!

jokes.text: 'Gigabytes.' [Laughing] They've done it again. 'Gigabytes.' Wait-This isn't Weird. Why, there's no magazine called Weird, is there? This is Wired. It's about computers and technology.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks 'Gigabytes' is a movie parody title
jokes.punchline: He slowly realizes he's been reading the wrong magazine entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Homer's confusion, with his dawning realization being funnier than the initial mistake

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Gigabytes? What kind of stupid movie title is that? Oh wait... this is a computer magazine.

jokes.text: The Internet? Is that thing still around?
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions the Internet as something new and exciting
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses it as if it's already passé
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of how quickly technology trends come and go, delivered with perfect Homer obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The Internet is something new and exciting!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The Internet? Bah! I'm already sick of it.

jokes.text: I know a Web site that shows monkeys doin' it.
jokes.setup: Discussion about the educational potential of the Internet
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately goes to the crude/sexual content
jokes.explanation: Typical Bart crudeness, somewhat predictable but economical

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The Internet? Ay caramba! Finally, a way to expand my mind and learn about the world!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Let me just type in... 'boobies.'

jokes.text: Bart, the Internet is more than a global pornography network. It's a- [Horn Honks]
jokes.setup: Lisa tries to defend the Internet's educational value
jokes.punchline: Her defense accidentally acknowledges it IS largely pornography
jokes.explanation: Smart joke where Lisa's attempt to be high-minded backfires perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The Internet is a valuable educational tool with vast resources for learning and research.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Then how come every time I use it, I end up looking at... uh... never mind.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, yes, there are some... inappropriate sites, but those are just a small fraction of—

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A small fraction? Lisa, I found like fifty of those in the first five minutes!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's... that's just because you were specifically looking for them!

jokes.text: Ach. Let's see what's been captured on the up-kilt camera.
jokes.setup: Willie is shown using a computer at the cybercafe
jokes.punchline: He's using it for voyeuristic purposes involving his own kilt
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor playing on Willie's Scottish stereotypes

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Ach, the internet! Finally, a use for these contraptions!

jokes.text: Ew. This lass needs a bit of grounds keeping. Eh? Aah! That's Willie!
jokes.setup: Willie comments on what he sees on the up-kilt camera
jokes.punchline: He realizes he's looking at himself
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation where Willie's criticism becomes self-discovery

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Och! What's this? Me own kilt!

jokes.text: My name is Mary. I'm 45, heavy and willing to settle for less.
jokes.setup: Willie is now apparently in a chat room or dating site
jokes.punchline: His pathetic dating profile description
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny portrayal of desperate online dating, enhanced by Willie's accent

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, listen up ye lonely lassies. Groundskeeper Willie here, lookin' fer a bonnie lass who appreciates a man with dirt under his fingernails and a heart full of Scottish rage. I enjoy long walks through the school grounds, tendin' to me grass, and yellin' at children. I'm a man of few words but many threats. If ye can handle a fella who smells like fertilizer and regret, then send me a message, ye daft woman!

jokes.text: Wow. This Mary's got the whole package.
jokes.setup: Mary's profile was clearly desperate and unappealing
jokes.punchline: Barney finds her attractive anyway
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate low standards joke for Barney

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: She's perfect! Look at those eyes, that smile... I'm in love!

jokes.text: I invested in something called News Corp- Dad! That's Fox! [Screams] Undo! Undo!
jokes.setup: Homer casually mentions investing in News Corp
jokes.punchline: Lisa panics because that's the network that owns their show
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about the show's own network ownership, with perfect panic from Lisa

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I just invested in News Corp. Great company!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, no! That's the network that owns this show!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So if the network fails, we all lose our jobs... including the animators drawing us right now!

jokes.text: I invested in something called News Corp- Dad! That's Fox! Undo! Undo!
jokes.setup: Homer casually mentions investing in News Corp
jokes.punchline: Lisa panics realizing he invested in Fox, the network that airs their show
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor about The Simpsons' own network with perfect character reactions

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I invested some money in News Corp.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you invested in Fox? The network that airs our show?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Do you realize what this means?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're profiting off our exploitation!

jokes.text: Oh, yes! Download to papa. [Beeping] Yoink dot adios, back-slash losers.
jokes.setup: Snake is robbing the cybercafe
jokes.punchline: He speaks in tech/internet terminology while committing the crime
jokes.explanation: Clever blend of tech jargon with Snake's criminal character

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Alright, dudes, this is a robbery. Nobody move or I'll drop you all from my friends list, man.

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Hand over all your bitcoins and ethernet cables, or I'm gonna DDoS this whole place.

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Heh heh, you could say I'm about to go viral with this heist, man.

jokes.text: It's just that you and Maude live like royalty in your fancy castle... while I got Marge trapped over there like a pig in a mud beehive.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to explain his financial desperation to Ned
jokes.punchline: The bizarre mixed metaphor 'pig in a mud beehive'
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully nonsensical Homer metaphor that somehow feels emotionally accurate

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ned, I'm in a real bind. I'm like a pig in a mud beehive.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: A pig in a mud beehive? Homer, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's how desperate I am, Ned. I'm mixing my metaphors!

jokes.text: [Chuckling] We picked those up cheap. They were evidence at a murder trial.
jokes.setup: Homer asks how Ned can afford nice appliances
jokes.punchline: Ned cheerfully reveals they're murder evidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion - wholesome Ned casually mentioning murder evidence with his usual cheer

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How can you afford all those nice appliances, Ned?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, they're murder evidence! The police auction them off real cheap!

jokes.text: He taught us how to live... a Burt Reynolds lifestyle on a Mac Davis income.
jokes.setup: Ned explains Chuck Garabedian's financial advice
jokes.punchline: The specific celebrity comparison between lifestyles and incomes
jokes.explanation: Clever reference joke that captures the essence of living beyond your means

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, Chuck Garabedian says if you live like a millionaire, you'll go broke. But if you live like a billionaire, you'll stay rich!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, it's simple economics, Homer. You see, billionaires can afford to live extravagantly without going broke.

jokes.text: Are you tired of missing out on the good things in life- Family vacations, jet packs, Solid Gold Dancers?
jokes.setup: Typical motivational speaker opening about life's pleasures
jokes.punchline: List escalates from reasonable (vacations) to absurd (jet packs, Solid Gold Dancers)
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with absurd 1980s references mixed with normal desires

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: Life is full of simple pleasures. A nice vacation, a good meal, spending time with loved ones.

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: But why stop there? Why not treat yourself to a jet pack? Solid Gold Dancers following you everywhere you go?

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: A personal blimp? A trained monkey butler? The sky's the limit, people!

jokes.text: Well, what do you expect? These yokels are pure Baltic Avenue.
jokes.setup: Someone complains about fat-cat bashing at the seminar
jokes.punchline: Using Monopoly property values to describe social class
jokes.explanation: Brilliant use of Monopoly as class metaphor - both snobbish and accurate

dialogue.speaker: Rich audience member
dialogue.line: I resent the implication that we fat cats are somehow less deserving than the rest of you!

dialogue.speaker: Rich audience member
dialogue.line: We earned our money the old-fashioned way — we inherited it.

dialogue.speaker: Rich audience member
dialogue.line: And another thing — I'm not a 'fat cat,' I'm more of a Boardwalk and Park Place kind of guy.

jokes.text: [Chuckles] Uh-oh! I'm late for the Short Line Railroad.
jokes.setup: Continues the Monopoly metaphor
jokes.punchline: He leaves to catch another Monopoly property reference
jokes.explanation: Nice continuation of the Monopoly bit, though not as strong as the setup

dialogue.speaker: Rich audience member
dialogue.line: He leaves to catch another Monopoly property reference

jokes.text: You see this tux? I got it cheap 'cause Roy Cohn died in it.
jokes.setup: Chuck is demonstrating how to save money
jokes.punchline: His tuxedo has a morbid history with a notorious figure
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's both specific and disturbing in the right way

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: Here's how to save money on formal wear. This tuxedo belonged to a very famous man.

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: Adolf Hitler.

jokes.text: And those beautiful women? They used to be men.
jokes.setup: Chuck is explaining how he gets everything cheap
jokes.punchline: Even his companions have a 'discount' backstory
jokes.explanation: Somewhat cheap shock humor, less sophisticated than the previous examples

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: Even my companions have a 'discount' backstory

jokes.text: Hey, I squeezed so hard it went into my hand.
jokes.setup: Everyone is squeezing pennies as instructed
jokes.punchline: Homer somehow managed to physically crush the penny
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy that shows Homer's excessive literal interpretation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why, I'm rich! I'm gonna buy myself a nice Duff beer... Oh wait, that costs money.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, you crushed your penny!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, and it felt great!

jokes.text: Ew. We're gonna do our grocery shopping at a 99-cent store? Well, maybe for your wedding.
jokes.setup: Lisa is disgusted by shopping at discount stores
jokes.punchline: Marge's dry comeback about Lisa's future economic prospects
jokes.explanation: Nice maternal putdown with good economic subtext

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mother, I can't believe you shop at these discount stores. The lighting is so fluorescent, and the products are of such poor quality.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well Lisa, when you're out on your own someday, you'll understand that sometimes you have to shop where you can afford to.

jokes.text: Look, Mom. They have your dress. [Groans] Thirty-three cents? I paid almost double that.
jokes.setup: Lisa points out Marge's dress is for sale cheaply
jokes.punchline: Marge reveals she overpaid even for discount clothing
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that makes Marge's frugality seem even more pathetic

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, I saw that dress on sale for $19.99.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I paid $39.99 for it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's twice the sale price!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I know. I bought it before it went on sale.

jokes.text: Sure, by their standards, but we live in America.
jokes.setup: Food expired by Mexican safety standards
jokes.punchline: Homer's patriotic justification for eating unsafe food
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of American exceptionalism applied to food safety

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just because it's expired by Mexican safety standards doesn't mean it's bad. It just means it's good enough for Americans!

jokes.text: Homer, you could have just unscrewed the bottom. A little late for 'could-haves,' Marge.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently destroyed their savings jar to get money out
jokes.punchline: His dismissive response to the obvious better solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - acknowledging better solutions while dismissing them

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why didn't you just ask me for the key?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A key? Why didn't I think of that?

jokes.text: Are you going through our garbage? That's right. You fat cats didn't finish your plankton. Now it's mine.
jokes.setup: Chuck appears at the restaurant
jokes.punchline: He's literally scavenging their leftover discount food
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how extreme penny-pinching becomes self-defeating

dialogue.speaker: Chuck Garabedian
dialogue.line: He's literally scavenging their leftover discount food

jokes.text: [With Jamaican Accent] No, mon, let's go home to Jamaica. I and I been in Babylon too long.
jokes.setup: Family is discussing potential vacation destinations
jokes.punchline: Bart suddenly adopts a full Rastafarian persona
jokes.explanation: Amusing random character shift, though somewhat disconnected

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yo, mon. We should be chillin' in Jamaica, ya know? All that reggae and the beaches and whatnot. It's like, the vibe, mon.

jokes.text: Oh, so Flanders thinks he can steal our vacation, huh?
jokes.setup: Ned getting the Japan seats they could have taken
jokes.punchline: Homer treats this as theft despite them not wanting Japan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer irrationality - angry about losing something he didn't want

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! Those were our seats! We could've gone to Japan!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Homer, we didn't want to go to Japan.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not the point! You stole our trip to Japan!

jokes.text: Sayonara, suckers.
jokes.setup: Taking the Japan flight he didn't want
jokes.punchline: Uses Japanese farewell while feeling victorious over Flanders
jokes.explanation: Good economy and irony - Homer feels like he won by taking a trip he didn't want

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sayonara, Flanders!

jokes.text: You liked Rashomon. That's not how I remember it.
jokes.setup: Marge reminds Homer he liked a Japanese film
jokes.punchline: Homer's response references the film's theme of subjective memory
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that perfectly captures Rashomon's central theme while being completely in character for Homer

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Remember, Homer? You loved that Japanese film we watched.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I don't remember it that way.

jokes.text: Besides, if we wanna see Japanese people we could have gone to the zoo.
jokes.setup: Homer continuing to complain about Japan
jokes.punchline: Casually racist suggestion about where to find Japanese people
jokes.explanation: Relies on racist stereotype, cheap shock humor that reveals character ignorance but not cleverly

jokes.text: Besides, if we wanna see Japanese people... we could have gone to the zoo. Homer! What? The guy who washes the elephants is Japanese.
jokes.setup: Homer makes a racist comparison
jokes.punchline: His 'defense' makes it even worse
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate ignorance but somewhat uncomfortable humor

jokes.text: Look, Mom. The safety instructions are written in haiku. 'Fasten seat belts tight. Your seat cushions float gently. Headsets, five dollars.'
jokes.setup: Lisa notices poetic safety instructions
jokes.punchline: The haiku ends with a mundane commercial message
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of both haiku tradition and airline safety announcements

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh, look at this! Safety instructions written as haiku. How delightful!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Exit to the left, / Emergency lights will guide, / Obey all our rules.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What an elegant way to convey such important—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh. It ends with 'Drink Coca-Cola.'

jokes.text: Sir, it is not safe to use electronic devices yet. You're the waitress.
jokes.setup: Flight attendant gives safety instruction
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissive and inappropriate response
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer rudeness and cultural insensitivity

dialogue.speaker: Flight Attendant
dialogue.line: In the event of a loss of cabin pressure, oxygen masks will automatically deploy from the compartment above your seat. Place the mask over your nose and mouth, secure it with the elastic band, and breathe normally.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. I'm just here for the peanuts.

jokes.text: Yeah, isn't it amazing? They're filming one right now in the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Jim Belushi movies
jokes.punchline: Implies he's making a crude bathroom movie
jokes.explanation: Moderately clever celebrity mockery, though somewhat predictable

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: Jim Belushi movies? More like Jim Belushi... bathroom.

jokes.text: Toga! Toga! Toga 2,000!
jokes.setup: Movie playing on flight
jokes.punchline: Sequel to Animal House set in the future
jokes.explanation: Simple satire of unnecessary sequels, not particularly inspired

dialogue.speaker: Flight Attendant
dialogue.line: We're showing the sequel to Animal House set in the future.

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: What's it called?

dialogue.speaker: Flight Attendant
dialogue.line: Animal House 2: The Retirement Years.

jokes.text: Welcome. I am honored to accept your waste.
jokes.setup: High-tech Japanese hotel room
jokes.punchline: The toilet speaks with formal politeness about bodily functions
jokes.explanation: Good culture clash humor between Japanese formality and bathroom functions

dialogue.speaker: Toilet
dialogue.line: Konnichiwa. I detect urgent need. Please be seated. I am honored to serve you. After you finish, I will provide gentle posterior cleansing with warm water at precisely 38 degrees Celsius. Thank you for using Premium Sanitation System 3000. Your comfort brings me great joy.

jokes.text: Isn't this that cartoon that causes seizures? [Beeping] [Grunting, Groaning]
jokes.setup: Bart recognizes a dangerous cartoon
jokes.punchline: The family immediately starts having seizures from watching it
jokes.explanation: Topical reference to real Pokemon seizure incident, well-executed physical comedy

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, that's Itchy & Scratchy: The Ultraviolent Years!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no, not that one!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Everyone cover your eyes!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: The family immediately starts having seizures from watching it

jokes.text: Robots be right back.
jokes.setup: Commercial break during seizure robot show
jokes.punchline: Broken English pun on 'we'll be right back'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay mixing robot theme with TV conventions

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: We'll be right back!

jokes.text: Dad, we didn't come halfway around the world to eat at Americatown. I'd like to see the Japanese take on the club sandwich. I bet it's smaller and more efficient.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants authentic Japanese experience
jokes.punchline: Homer's stereotypical expectations about Japanese efficiency applied to sandwiches
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic mixing cultural stereotypes with mundane concerns

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I want to experience authentic Japanese culture.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, Japanese efficiency! They'll make your sandwich in like 2 seconds.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's not what authentic Japanese culture is about.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure it is. I saw it on TV. A guy made 500 sushi rolls while I was still chewing my first bite.

jokes.text: Hey, you know, I once knew a man from Nantucket. And? Let's just say the stories about him are greatly exaggerated.
jokes.setup: Moe starts the famous limerick setup
jokes.punchline: Instead of the crude punchline, he deflates it with disappointment
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of a well-known dirty joke format

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: There once was a man from Peru

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Who dreamed he was eating his shoe

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: He woke with a fright to find that the night had turned golden, not lime colored... and he was deeply disappointed.

jokes.text: Howdy, gangstas. I am average American Joe salary-man waiter.
jokes.setup: American-themed restaurant in Japan
jokes.punchline: Waiter's awkward attempt at American slang and identity
jokes.explanation: Good satire of cultural misunderstanding and stereotyping

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Waiter
dialogue.line: Welcome to American-themed restaurant! I am very American. I eat hot dog for breakfast, watch baseball, and say 'how you doin'?' to everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Waiter
dialogue.line: Also, I have gun in holster and I say 'yippee-ki-yay' even though I don't know what it means.

jokes.text: Don't ask me. I don't know anything. I am product of American education system. I also build poor-quality cars and inferior-style electronics.
jokes.setup: Customer asks about Japanese food options
jokes.punchline: Waiter satirizes American stereotypes about their own products
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire turning American stereotypes back on themselves

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: What kind of Japanese food do you have?

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Waiter
dialogue.line: We have sushi, ramen, tempura, yakitori...

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Do you have anything American?

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course! We have Big Mac, Bud Light, and high fructose corn syrup. Very authentic American cuisine.

jokes.text: Oh, my goodness. Homer, those are $150. It's worth every cent. I'm tired of fumbling with round fruit. D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer wants to buy expensive square watermelon
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous justification about round fruit being inconvenient
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - creating a problem that doesn't exist to justify excess spending

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I'm buying this square watermelon.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's ridiculous. It costs three times as much!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, think about it. Round watermelons are inconvenient. They roll all over the place. They take up too much room in the fridge. But this square one? Perfect. It sits nice and neat.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's the dumbest thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dumb? Or genius? I'm buying it.

jokes.text: So many rice crackers claim to be low-cal... but only Fujikawa rice crackers make your interiors go bananas. What did I do to deserve this? Oh, right.
jokes.setup: Woody Allen doing a Japanese commercial
jokes.punchline: His self-aware commentary about his career choices
jokes.explanation: Excellent celebrity satire that captures Woody Allen's neurotic self-reflection

dialogue.speaker: Woody Allen
dialogue.line: I'm doing a Japanese commercial. My career has really hit bottom. Next week I'm gonna be the spokesman for a suppository.

jokes.text: What did I do to deserve this? Oh, right.
jokes.setup: Woody Allen asking about his fate
jokes.punchline: Remembers his real-life scandals
jokes.explanation: Sharp meta-commentary on celebrity scandals with perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Woody Allen
dialogue.line: Wait, what happens to me?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say your personal life makes mine look like a Disney movie.

dialogue.speaker: Woody Allen
dialogue.line: Oh no...

jokes.text: Like we say in my county, 'Hasta la vista, baby.'
jokes.setup: Homer has just disrupted a sumo wrestling match
jokes.punchline: He uses a Spanish/Arnold Schwarzenegger catchphrase as if it's local wisdom
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer cultural confusion, though the reference is somewhat dated

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll be back.

jokes.text: Congratulations. I am the emperor. Yeah? And I'm 'clobber-saurus.'
jokes.setup: The emperor congratulates Homer on his sumo victory
jokes.punchline: Homer's made-up title mixing violence with dinosaurs
jokes.explanation: Good Homer invention that sounds both childish and intimidating

dialogue.speaker: Emperor
dialogue.line: You have won great honor. What title would you like?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh... Lord of the Rippers... and the Dinosaurs!

jokes.text: All hail Emperor Clobbersaurus.
jokes.setup: Homer's ridiculous self-given title
jokes.punchline: Crowd formally accepts his absurd name
jokes.explanation: Good callback with formal treatment of Homer's childish name

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: From now on, call me 'The Scopalami'.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: All hail The Scopalami!

jokes.text: In jail we had to be in this dumb Kabuki play about the 47 Ronin and I wanted to be Yoshi, but they made me Ori.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about Japanese experiences
jokes.punchline: Petty complaint about character assignment in classic Japanese drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer pettiness about high culture, treating serious drama as personal slight

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I spent three weeks in Japan, and you know what bothered me the most? In those old samurai movies, they always give the best roles to the Japanese guys!

jokes.text: Don't worry, ichiban. I'll show you something Japanese.
jokes.setup: Homer wants to do something Japanese for Lisa
jokes.punchline: He uses Japanese word incorrectly while promising authenticity
jokes.explanation: Mild humor about Homer's cultural appropriation attempts

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Lisa. I'm gonna do something very Japanese for you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll make it completely authentic. Bonsai!

jokes.text: Beats me. Try getting a job and earning some money. That's what I did. By the way, 'ambassador' is taken.
jokes.setup: Homer asking for help getting home
jokes.punchline: Ambassador offers no help but protects his job title
jokes.explanation: Good satire of bureaucratic indifference with petty job protection

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Please, I just need to get home to my family in Springfield.

dialogue.speaker: US Ambassador
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but there's nothing I can do to help you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But you're the US Ambassador! Isn't that your job?

dialogue.speaker: US Ambassador
dialogue.line: My job is to protect the title 'US Ambassador.' Actually helping Americans? That's not in my job description.

jokes.text: Knife goes in, guts come out. Knife goes in, guts come out.
jokes.setup: Homer working at fish processing job
jokes.punchline: Reduces complex work to simple, repetitive mantra
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer reduction of complexity to simple terms, highly quotable rhythm

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Work, work, work. That's all it is. Day after day, the same thing over and over.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I've figured it out. It's really just one simple thing repeated.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Squeeze, gut, squeeze, gut, squeeze, gut...

jokes.text: Knife goes in, guts come out. Knife goes in, guts come out. Spare my life and I will grant you three wi- [Screams] Knife goes in, guts come out.
jokes.setup: Homer has found his calling in fish gutting
jokes.punchline: He continues his mantra even when a fish tries to negotiate
jokes.explanation: Dark absurdist humor with good escalation and memorable repetition

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fish guts, fish guts, fish guts!

dialogue.speaker: Fish
dialogue.line: Wait, wait! I have a family!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fish guts, fish guts, fish guts!

jokes.text: Well, I haven't talked it over with the family- [Chuckles] but I think we'd all like a free dinner at Americatown.
jokes.setup: Game show host asks what the family wishes for
jokes.punchline: Homer chooses the most mundane possible prize
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Homer's consistent poor priorities

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: What does your family wish for?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A new car!

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: Wonderful! Anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nope. Well, maybe some donuts.

jokes.text: Your shows reward knowledge. We punish ignorance. Ignor-What? [Screams]
jokes.setup: Host explains their game show philosophy
jokes.punchline: Homer proves his ignorance immediately by not understanding 'ignorance'
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup where Homer immediately demonstrates the concept being explained

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: On this show, we celebrate ignorance.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ignorance? I don't know what that means.

jokes.text: Is the answer 'Japan'? Actually, it is.
jokes.setup: Homer gives what seems like a random guess
jokes.punchline: His obvious answer is somehow correct
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expected game show failure

dialogue.speaker: Game show host
dialogue.line: What is the capital of Assyria?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh... Springfield?

dialogue.speaker: Game show host
dialogue.line: That is correct!

jokes.text: He seems okay but he is being burned internally.
jokes.setup: Homer appearing fine after lightning round
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of internal burning
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with medical detachment, good contrast with Homer's apparent wellness

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Assistant
dialogue.line: You're doing great! How are you feeling?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Oh, I feel fine. A little internal burning, but fine.

jokes.text: If that's Homer, then who the hell's been puttin' beers on his tab? D'oh. Whoo-hoo. Uh-That boy ain't right.
jokes.setup: Moe sees Homer on Japanese TV
jokes.punchline: Realizes someone's been impersonating Homer at the bar
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing the bar's dysfunction continues without Homer

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... that's Homer on Japanese TV!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: But Homer's been here at the bar this whole time... hasn't he?

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Uh, Moe... I hate to break it to ya...

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That ain't been Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: What?!

jokes.text: All you have to do is to pick them up from inside that volcano.
jokes.setup: Winning airline tickets
jokes.punchline: Final challenge is literally impossible/deadly
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation of game show dangers, though somewhat predictable

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: You've won airline tickets! Now for our final challenge...

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: You must safely land this Boeing 747 while it's on fire and losing altitude!

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: Ready? Go!

jokes.text: I sure hope they don't have an ice cream round. [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer comments hopefully about future game show rounds
jokes.punchline: Immediately gets hurt by ice cream, proving his hope was misplaced
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic timing where stating hope immediately leads to disappointment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Maybe next time I'll win a prize that doesn't hurt me.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm, ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Owww! My teeth!

jokes.text: I sure hope they don't have an ice cream round.
jokes.setup: Homer being tortured on game show
jokes.punchline: Expresses fear of ice cream round while being electrocuted
jokes.explanation: Good irony - Homer fears pleasure while experiencing pain, shows his twisted priorities

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, not the ice cream round! Anything but the ice cream round!

jokes.text: Don't worry, that 'lava' is just orangeade... made by our sponsor, Osaka Orangeade Concern. It burns! It burns! It's loaded with wasabi.
jokes.setup: Host reveals the lava is fake orangeade
jokes.punchline: The orangeade is still painful due to wasabi
jokes.explanation: Good double fake-out where the relief is immediately undercut

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: The lava is actually just orangeade!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Orangeade? But it still hurts!

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: Yes, we added wasabi.

jokes.text: Don't worry, that 'lava' is just orangeade made by our sponsor, Osaka Orangeade Concern.
jokes.setup: Family appearing to die in lava
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's fake and plugs sponsor
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with satire of product placement in dangerous situations

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: And now, the Simpson family appears to be dying in a vat of lava!

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: But it's all fake! This segment is brought to you by Duff Beer.

jokes.text: It burns! It burns! It's loaded with wasabi.
jokes.setup: Revelation that lava is fake orangeade
jokes.punchline: Still painful because it contains wasabi
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - subverts the relief by making the 'safe' option still painful

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me this lava is just fake orangeade?

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: That's right.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, that's a relief. I thought I was really dying.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: Oh, you still are. It contains wasabi.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: AHHHHHHH!

jokes.text: Game shows aren't about cruelty. They're about greed and wonderful prizes... like poorly built catamarans. But somewhere along the line, you lost your way.
jokes.setup: Homer gives a speech about game show values
jokes.punchline: His 'noble' vision still includes terrible prizes and greed
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire where Homer's moral stance still accepts the underlying problems

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, a game show should represent the highest ideals of American values: honesty, integrity, and the pursuit of—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: —a new Cadillac and a year's supply of Rice-A-Roni!

jokes.text: Good-bye, Japan. I'll miss your Kentucky Fried Chicken... and your sparkling, whale-free seas.
jokes.setup: Homer gives a farewell to Japan
jokes.punchline: He'll miss American chain restaurants and makes a backhanded environmental comment
jokes.explanation: Good Homer sendoff that shows he learned nothing and maintains his backwards priorities

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll miss you, Japan. But not as much as I'll miss the American chain restaurants back home.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: At least when we destroy the environment, we do it with bacon.

jokes.text: Folks, we're experiencing some moderate Godzilla-related turbulence at this time.
jokes.setup: Turbulence on flight from Japan
jokes.punchline: Casual announcement about giant monster attack
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery treating kaiju attack as routine airline inconvenience, perfect economy

jokes.text: Folks, we're experiencing... some moderate Godzilla-related turbulence at this time, so I'm gonna go ahead... and ask you to put your seat belts back on.
jokes.setup: Pilot makes routine turbulence announcement
jokes.punchline: The turbulence is caused by a giant monster, treated matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery making a kaiju attack sound routine

dialogue.speaker: Pilot
dialogue.line: We're experiencing some light turbulence. Nothing to worry about.

dialogue.speaker: Pilot
dialogue.line: We've got a giant monster on the left wing. Maintenance has been notified.

tmdb_overview: After their bank account is drained at a cyber-cafe, the Simpsons go on a disastrous low-budget trip to Japan, where they wind up as contestants on a humiliating game show.
insight: Absurdist momentum sustains 69 jokes across travel chaos, though impact dips below season standard.
